findings.description: range(total_chunks - 1) skips the final chunk, so every assembled file is missing its last chunk and is corrupt.
session.filename is joined into UPLOAD_DIR with no sanitization, allowing path traversal or absolute-path escape (e.g. '../../etc/x.pdf') despite the extension allowlist.
upload_id is interpolated into chunk file paths without validation, so a crafted id with path separators can escape CHUNK_DIR on write.
Two sessions sharing the same filename write to the same dest_path, overwriting each other's output with no collision handling.
receive_chunk writes arbitrary data with no per-chunk or cumulative size bound, so the documented MAX_FILE_MB limit is never enforced and uploads are effectively unbounded (DoS/disk exhaustion).
file_size_ok / MAX_FILE_MB enforcement is never invoked by assemble or receive, so the size cap is dead code and oversized files are stored.
verify_checksum reads the entire (up to 200MB+) file into memory with f.read() instead of hashing in streamed blocks.
_sessions dict and per-session received set are mutated with no locking, racing under the concurrent requests typical of an upload endpoint.
assemble has no exception handling; a missing/unreadable chunk raises mid-write, leaving a partial dest file and orphaned session plus chunk files (cleanup never runs).
start() overwrites any existing session for the same upload_id, silently discarding prior received state and orphaning already-written chunk files on disk.
Validation relies solely on file extension; file content/MIME is never checked, so disallowed payloads can be uploaded under an allowed extension.
Chunk write is not atomic (no temp-file-plus-rename or fsync), so a crash mid-write can leave a corrupt chunk on disk.
del self._sessions[upload_id] can raise KeyError if the session is removed concurrently (e.g. via abort) between the is_complete check and the delete.
Checksum compared with != rather than hmac.compare_digest; low impact since a SHA-256 digest is not a secret, but constant-time compare would be defensive.
Storage paths and limits are hardcoded module-level constants instead of being configurable/injectable.
Optional is imported and used for the return type while the file otherwise targets modern typing; 'str | None' would be more consistent.
